tossups.question: A character who leaves one of these places to look for the woman he loves finds her dying in a hotel, so he crossdresses and gets hired as her nurse. In a feminist novel titled for one of these places, Gregory Rose agrees to submit to Lyndall so she will marry him. A pig-iron businessman is appalled when the body of a mysterious man is dumped in one of these places before being unearthed by a (*) flood. An Olive Schreiner novel is titled for one of these places in Africa. The owner of one of these places has an affair with Denys Finch-Hatton, a big-game hunter who dies in a plane crash. The failure of one of these places caused the author of “Babette’s Feast” to return to Denmark. For 10 points, name this kind of place where Karen Blixen set Out of Africa.

tossups.answer: farms [or coffee plantations; accept The Story of an African Farm; prompt on estates] (The third sentence is from The Conservationist by Nadine Gordimer.)

tossups.question: In a novel by this author, the author of a report on education contacts the Bashi former PIP leader Edward Shinza. This author described the Gala speaker Mweta (“muh-WAY-tah”) inviting Evelyn James Bray back to his country after gaining independence in A Guest of Honour. The gay teenager Terry hitchhikes to avoid conscription in a novel by this author whose protagonist harasses a Portuguese woman on a flight. A character created by this author abandons Conrad to enter a relationship with (*) Bernard Chabalier. In a novel by this author, an unknown Black man is buried on the farm that Mehring just bought. This author depicted the life of Rosa, whose father Lionel was a communist and anti-apartheid activist. For 10 points, name this author of The Conservationist and Burger’s Daughter.

tossups.question: In a story by this author, a girl named for Charlotte Corday from the play Marat/Sade learns about the affair of her mother, the deceased actress Laila de Morne. In another story by this author, the narrator gifts a suit to the father of a farmhand who asks for a refund of 20 pounds after the wrong body is exhumed from a grave. This author of "A Beneficiary" and "Six Feet of the Country" wrote a novel in which a man is scolded after driving into town to buy supplies and batteries for a radio. Daniel steals a shotgun which was earlier used to shoot two warthogs in that novel by this author, which ends as Maureen runs towards a helicopter. For 10 points, name this author who created the Smales family in her anti-Apartheid novel July's People.

tossups.question: In a short story by this author, Mrs. Bamjee is arrested for distributing leaflets and wears the title “Chip of Glass Ruby.” In a novel by this author, the protagonist leverages her connection to Brandt Vermeulen to get a passport to visit France, where she meets Katya. One of this author’s characters imagines a conversation with Jacobus, the foreman who had found a corpse on his (*) farm. In another novel by this author, a village boy stares into the “blue-steel tunnels” of an object that is later suspected to be stolen by Daniel. In that novel, the disappearance of a bakkie prompts Maureen to confront a former servant. For 10 points, name this writer who examined the conditions of apartheid in their novels The Conservationist, Burger’s Daughter, and July’s People.

tossups.question: In a story by this author, a couple places a plaque reading “YOU HAVE BEEN WARNED” on their front gate in a series of escalating security measures, which culminate in their son dying when he climbs into razor wire. The protagonist considers Pushkin’s frizzy hair and a photo of his own prospector great-grandfather in a story by this author in which Frederick Morris fails in his search for dark-skinned ancestors, after hearing a comment on the radio about a (*) composer’s African heritage. This author of the stories “Once Upon a Time” and “Beethoven Was One-Sixteenth Black” wrote a novel in which Bam teaches Daniel how to use a gun, while a servant drives a bakkie that used to belong to the Smales family, who flee from Johannesburg. For 10 points, name this South African author of July’s People.

tossups.question: One playwright from this country followed seven different points of view in the 1984 play Black Dog, which starred another playwright from this country who wrote Have You Seen Zandile? An old man invited to a campfire in one play from this country is refused wine by one character but told stories by another until he mysteriously dies. That play from this country describes the wandering after the home of (*) Boesman and Lena was bulldozed. In another play from this country, a character’s job as a watchkeeper in a park leaves him with sore feet that his companion prepares hot water baths to soothe. That play from this country describes the title bond between Morris and Zachariah who live in a shack in the “colored” section of town. For 10 points, name this country for which Barney Simon and other playwrights wrote plays criticizing policies like apartheid.
tossups.answer: South Africa [or Republic of South Africa or RSA] (The unnamed playwrights are, in order, Gcina Mhlophe, Nadine Gordimer, and Athol Fugard.)

tossups.question: In a story titled for one of these animals from the collection Beethoven was One-Sixteenth Black, the narrator watches one of them die while working at her typewriter. A crablike animal who is boiled and then escapes a house without his legs was created as a reference to one of these animals in the story “Father’s Last Escape” by Bruno Schulz. Another book refers to one of these animals as “ungeziefer,” or “unfit for sacrifice.” A couple discusses their (*) “young and voluptuous” daughter on a tram ride after the death of one of these animals, who prizes a picture of a woman in furs. Upon awakening from “uneasy dreams,” one of these animals is supplied with food by his sister Grete. For 10 points, Gregor Samsa transforms into what kind of animal in Franz Kafka’s The Metamorphosis?
tossups.answer: cockroaches [accept bugs or insects or vermin; accept dung beetles] (The lead-in refers to “Gregor” by Nadine Gordimer.)

tossups.question: This author asserted that “caches of old papers are like graves; you shouldn’t open them” to open her story “A Beneficiary.” In another story by this author, Rad plants explosives in his lover Vera’s luggage, resulting in an explosion on a plane. A farm boy is given a stranger’s corpse instead of his brother’s corpse in a story by this author of “Some Are Born to Sweet Delight.” This author of “Six Feet of the Country” wrote the story collection Beethoven Was One-Sixteenth Black. The protagonist of a novel by this author sees a Life magazine photograph depicting the servant girl Lydia carrying books. A yellow truck known as a “bakkie” appears in that novel by this author that ends with Bam’s wife Maureen running towards a helicopter. For 10 points, name this author of July’s People.

packet.name: A - Columbia A and Texas A

tossups.question: In one story by this author, a pregnant woman dies when her fiancé sneaks a bomb into her luggage, through a last-minute gift, killing everyone on board her flight. A novel by this author begins with a man watching children gather around a clutch of “pale freckled eggs” and includes a scene where the protagonist listens to the howling of a beaten dog on Christmas Day. This author of “Some Are Born to Sweet Delight” created a character who muses on a photo of a white schoolgirl and black woman that appears in a Life coffee-table book. That novel by this author ends with Maureen running towards a helicopter and is about the title servant helping the Smales family escape a black revolution. For 10 points, name this South African author of The Conservationist and July’s People.

tossups.question: One of this author’s novels chronicles the disintegrating marriage of Vera Stark, who takes a government position and forces her husband Bennet to join a luggage company. In one novel by this author, Julie builds a relationship with the deported auto mechanic Abdu after visiting his home country and becoming enamored with his family. One of this author’s characters carries on an affair with the liberal Antonia Mancebo and is constantly disturbed by the image of a body found on his (*) farm. This creator of the industrialist Mehring wrote the short story collection Beethoven Was One-Sixteenth Black. In another novel by this author, Maureen abandons her family to flee in a helicopter after Daniel, a friend of the title servant, steals Bamford Smales’ gun to join the black revolutionaries. For 10 points, name this South African author of The Conservationist and July’s People.

tossups.question: This author wrote a story in which a chicken breeder on a farm vainly tries to recover the 25 pounds he spent exhuming a corpse that turned out not to be his brother’s body. In another story, a woman yells at her new husband for making a vendor run afer their train and ofer half price for a beautifully-carved lion with real fur. This writer interspersed quotes from the Reverend Henry Callaway between chapters of a novel that opens as a circle of children disobey a landowner by playing with eleven freckled guinea-fowl eggs. This author of “Six Feet of the Country” wrote a novel in which hypocritical lefies at the L.A. Cafe mock Julie Summers for dating an illegal immigrant with an economics degree, an Arab mechanic named Abdu. This author wrote a novel in which the homosexual Terry gives clothes to the workers on his father’s 400-acre farm where Jacobus buries the corpse of an unknown black man that comes to haunt the title white businessman, Mehring. For 10 points, name this South-African author of The Pickup who wrote The Conservationist and July’s People.

In another novel by this author, Baasie criticizes the legacy of the title character’s father Lionel, prompting Rosa to return from France. bonuses.leadin: The 3,000-stanza epic Utenzi wa Vita vya Uhuru, which chronicles World War II in the style of this country’s poetry, is the magnum opus of Shaaban bin Robert, this country’s national poet. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this modern-day country, where Yusuf runs after a column of conscripted men at the start of World War I after returning with Uncle Aziz’s caravan at the end of one novel.
This South African author discussed the history of Tanzania in an essay for The Atlantic. This author also wrote Burger’s Daughter and July’s People.
Ilyas searches for his uncle in Germany after World War II at the end of this author’s novel Afterlives. This Tanzanian-born author of Paradise won the 2021 Nobel Prize in Literature.
answers: Tanzania [or United Republic of Tanzania or Jamhuri ya Muungano wa Tanzania] (The novel is Paradise.)

This other member of the “Sixtiers” and frequent collaborator of Ingrid Jonker described his time in prison in The True Confessions of an Albino Terrorist.

A time period described in Mongane Wally Serote's "For Don M - Banned" titles this novel by Sestiger Andre Brink, in which Captain Stolz impedes Ben du Toit and Melanie Bruwer's investigation of Gordon Ngubene's murder.
Afrikaner lawyer Bram Fischer inspired this author to write about a Communist activist who raises Baasie alongside his white daughter Rosa, but dies in prison, in Burger's Daughter.

bonuses.leadin: Harvard University Press now publishes the Loeb Classical Library that was first published by this company. For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this British publisher whose educational wing printed Leon Comber’s Writing in Asia Series and James Currey’s Caribbean Writers Series. Both were modeled on this publisher’s African Writers Series.
In 1994, this author was appointed as editorial advisor to the Heinemann African Writers Series. This Zanzibar-born author of By the Sea and Paradise won the 2021 Nobel Prize in Literature.
The series published this author’s short story collection Some Monday for Sure in 1976. This South African author is better known for novels such as The Conservationist and July’s People.

bonuses.leadin: This author’s early short stories were collected in Six Feet of the Country, whose title story involves the discovery of a corpse on a farm, like her later novel The Conservationist. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author who defended her fiction in What Happened to Burger’s Daughter or How South African Censorship Works.

In this Gordimer story, white professor Frederick Morris hears the title claim on classical music radio and wonders about his own somewhat dark-skinned great-grandfather. It titles a 2007 collection that includes “A Beneficiary”.
The menacing title of this Gordimer novella alludes both to a baboon stalking the white suburbs of Johannesburg and to a multiracial four-person terrorist cell. It titles a 1984 collection.

bonuses.leadin: In a story by this author, the farm worker Petrus pays the morgue for the body of his illegal immigrant brother, only to be given the wrong corpse to bury. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author of the story “Six Feet of the Country.” A similar situation arises in a novel by this author which incorporates Zulu myth to describe how Jacobus finds the body of a Black man on Mehring’s farm.
Gordimer’s works, like the aforementioned novel The Conservationist, often oppose this policy, according to which White and Black people were segregated in her native South Africa.
A white apartheid activist tries to prove his own Black heritage in a Gordimer story titled for how “[this historical figure] was One-Sixteenth Black,” which titles a 2008 collection by her.
answers_sanitized: Ludwig van Beethoven [accept Beethoven was One-Sixteenth Black]

bonuses.leadin: Prem Bodasingh refuses to give up her seat on the bus in one scene in this novel. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this fictional reworking of Alan Paton’s years as an activist, including real characters such as Helen Joseph. The title of this novel refers to the reaction of foreign visitors observing South African politics.
Julie Summers' car breaks down in this other author’s novel The Pickup. This writer’s other novels include The Conservationist.

Another novel by Gordimer ends with Maureen Smales running towards an unmarked one of these vehicles that ‘may contain saviors or murderers’. This vehicle arrives at the village where the liberal white Smales family has fled from race war in Johannesburg.
answers_sanitized: Ah, But Your Land is Beautiful

bonuses.leadin: A character who is reluctant to finish this book remembers Lydia’s baking after reading this book’s accounts of 1628 bread riots. For 10 points each:

bonuses.leadin: In one short story by this author, Vera dates a foreign man named Rad who eventually plants explosives in her luggage in order to blow up a plane. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author whose first work was the short story collection Face To Face. In addition to short stories including “Some are Born to Sweet Delight”, this author wrote a short novel in which Maureen Smales refuses to finish reading a copy of Manzoni's The Betrothed.
In this Gordimer short story from the collection A Soldier's Embrace, the village of Dilolo is burned after its chief reveals that it is harboring guerrillas during the Rhodesian Bush War.

In Gordimer's short story “The Life of the Imagination”, the narrator is anxious that black men will kill her due to anger over this set of policies, which Gordimer agitated against as part of the African National Congress.

bonuses.leadin: Binyavanga Wainaina’s satirical essay “How to Write About Africa” informs budding writers that, “after celebrity activists and aid workers,” people with this profession are “Africa’s most important people.” For 10 points each:
parts: Identify this profession of people who, according to Wainana, work to preserve “Africa’s rich heritage.” A novel titled for a member of this profession is set on a 400-acre farm administered by the foreman Jacobus.

Wainaina advises writers to always use either the word “Africa,”“Darkness,” or this one in the title of a book. The narrator of Hemingway’s “The Snows of Kilimanjaro” dies of gangrene during one of these hunting trips.

Wainaina concludes his essay by suggesting that books about Africa should contain a nightclub with this name where “prostitutes and guerillas” hang out. Cyprian Ekwensi’s Jagua Nana works at one such nightclub in Lagos.
answers: conservationists [or The Conservationist] (The novel is by Nadine Gordimer.)
answers_sanitized: conservationists [or The Conservationist] (The novel is by Nadine Gordimer.)

bonuses.leadin: This essay notes that using words such as “shadow,” “drum,” “sun,” or “bygone” in subtitles is helpful in the title activity. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this satirical essay by Binyavanga Wainaina that recommends an “I-expected-so-much” tone and a closing quote about “rainbows or renaissances” by Nelson Mandela to those doing the title action.

“How to Write About Africa” was originally written as a letter of complaint to this British literary magazine, where it was later published. This magazine was re-launched under the editorship of Bill Buford in 1979 after originally being founded by Cambridge students in the nineteenth century.

This author’s short story “Beethoven Was One-Sixteenth Black” appeared alongside “How to Write About Africa” in a 2005 issue of Granta. That same year, this author published a novel about the environmental activist Paul Bannerman titled Get a Life.

bonuses.leadin: This book was initially banned in South Africa, but was unbanned three months later. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this novel in which Rosa deals with the legacy of her father, Lionel, and with the anti-white militant activism of her childhood friend Baasie.
Burger’s Daughter was written by this female South African of The Conservationist and July’s People.

The Burger family was based loosely on the family of this South African anti-apartheid activist, who led the defense at the Rivonia Trial. Gordimer also wrote a 1961 essay entitled “Why Did [this man] Choose To Go to Jail?”.

bonuses.leadin: This novel concerns the story of the adult orphan Rosa, whose anti-apartheid parents were members of the Communist party. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this novel in which the title character leaves South Africa for the south of France after being unable to prevent a black man from violently beating a donkey in Soweto.
Burger’s Daughter is a novel by this South African author of July’s People and The Conservationist.

This character is Rosa’s childhood friend who was taken in by the Burgers after his father died. When Rosa meets this character again many years later he is dismissive of her for her father’s sacrifices which were no greater than those made in the same fight by black people.
answers_sanitized: Zwelinzima Vulindlela (accept either, also accept Baasie)

parts: This woman described the Hunter’s Allegory and created the awesomely-named villain Bonaparte Blenkins in a novel published in 1883, The Story of an African Farm.
Rosa struggles with the legacy of her Communist father in this woman’s novel Burger’s Daughter.
Olive Schreiner and Nadine Gordimer were both white. The first English novel written by a black South African was this “Epic of South African Life” by the inimitable Sol Plaatje.

bonuses.leadin: Answer some questions about South African short stories, for 10 points each.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer some questions about South African short stories, for 10 points each.
parts: This title story of a collection tells of a mission to kill a man called Baden-Powell. On the way, soldiers lose faith in their commander after he has to ask black men for directions. Stephanus Van Barnevelt explains that he would risk court-martial and refuse to return to the title location of this short story, unless it also meant surrendering to the English.

“Mafeking Road” is by this author of Jacaranda in the Night who was put on death row for shooting and killing his stepbrother during an argument. He told the story of his time in prison in Cold Stone Jug.
This contemporary South African Nobel Prize winner published the short story collection A Soldier's Embrace, but is better known for novels such as The Conservationist.

bonuses.leadin: This character reveals that she drowned some kittens in a bucket of water. FTPE:
parts: Name this woman, who was once photographed as a child with her servant, Lydia, carrying her school case. She's first seen arriving with her husband at a native village and ends the novel by running towards a mysterious helicopter.
Maureen Smales appears in July’s People, a novel by this South African Nobel laureate.
In this 1987 Gordimer novel, Kim Capran renames herself “Hillela” and marries a black member of the ANC, who ends up becoming the fictitious first black president of South Africa.
